quotes.quote: Why can't I take a full swing on the driver without slicing? If I take a lot slower swing it goes straight(about 250 yards) but when I swing faster it slices. The harder I swing the more it slices. I feel i'm leaving some distance by slowing the swing and would like to be free to take a full back swing and accellerate as fast as possible through the swing, what do I need to do? I don't think my swing angle or mechanics changer when I swing hard, it just slices more.

I cannot judge actually without seeing, of course ... but it SOUNDS to me like you're coming over the top - your first move down is 'casting' with your arms. Coming over the top opens the club face (slice-er-roni). The faster you swing the more the face opens, I'm guessing.
I think that when you try to hit it harder there is a good chance you are gripping the club tighter which wont let your hands come through.

swinging hard usually means swing top-down instead of bottom up and trying to generate extra power through your shoulders and hands instead of through your legs and hips. Almost all great golf swing starts form the bottom up with hips rotation seeting the speed and the rest of the body following. my guess is that in your hard and easy swings, your hips are moving at close to the same speed. As a result, when the upper body moves faster the club has nowhere to go but an outside in swing path

i used to have the same prob if i swung hard i would slice i think it was cuz if i gripped to hard it would make my wrist turn the club face different but now i just do a nice slow and steady drive and get it straight usually

This is the siren call we all should ignore but rarely do: more distance off the tee; booming 280yd+ drives. I'm more guilty at this than most, and the irony is that when I swing 'within myself' I'm farther down the fairway and in better position. But it's deceptive. If you're 240 and in the fairway where would you be if you really swung the club as hard as you can? I've had to accept that fact that I'm not a long hitter of the ball.

Your arms and body get out of synch when you swing harder. In any case, if you can hit it 250 and straight with an easy swing, you hit it it better than 90% of players, despite the boasts of those who 'regularly' hit it 300 but in actual fact don't realise that thy hit hit it 220, so keep swing slowly. A harder swing rarely gives a better result.

we're all men... once we get on the first tee box... everything we practiced on the range goes out the window.... we try and become the long driving champ... i know thats how i am sometimes... GRIP IT AND RIP IT!!! gotta learn to start with a more relaxed approach...

I'm not seeing the results. I'm 12 sessions in. My initial driver speed was 92 mph. My green speed max since is 103, blue is 98 and red is 96. All very close to my actual driver speed. What am I doing wrong. Above person with 100mph swings green at 118-120. I'm not even close to that percentage jump.

I recently purchased superspeed training sticks and have begun the first phase. I'm concerned that, when i swing the superspeed sticks as hard as possible, i'm casting (and creating a huge gap between my trail elbow and my side). When I slow down a bit, it doesn't happen.

I mean, it seems like a silly product. You swing a weighted club and over year's time you'll see a ~8-10% increase in clubhead speed. There's no difference to just going to work out for a year and working the golf swing muscles. This 'system' is basically just a year long exercise routine that works the golf swing muscles so obviously you'll increase in speed.

I was swinging about 110 5 yrs ago and now I am at 99 and it is frustrating. I am 59 and play 1-2 times a week and practice at least 3 times a week. Hoping to get close to the 110 I was or at least 107 to be competitive.

I have varied swing speed which I do not completely understand. Maybe it's because I do not play often enough or my motor units cannot fire effectively unless I'm primed to swing efficiently; I know I swing best if I ramp up into my swing with 10-20 swings before playing

My SS is around 101mph and I brought these to the sim the other night... I was able to get 138mph my fastest to date with the light stick and 122mph with the heavy. Than I teed one up and I got 105mph on trackman. Why is there such a big drop off from swinging the heavy to actually swinging my driver??? Is it air resistance or is my swing more sequenced with the sticks because I'm not trying to hit a ball???

It definitely works. I'm scratch and had 108 chs. I got up to 117 with the training in just a few weeks. However, in my case, I became flippy. I found I couldn't maintain my drive and hold golf swing that was mainly pivot driven. I developed way too much flip/roll release to generate the speed. So I stopped the training bc my accuracy fell off badly.

Used the SuperSpeed and followed the training protocol strictly for about two months. My swing speed definitely increased. A good hit of my driver goes 10-15 yards further. However, the biggest problem is it will break your tempo and rhythm since you will try to swing and hit as hard as possible. I drive the ball further on good contacts, but my bad contacts also increase dramatically. By the end of day, my score suffers.

None of this so-called information is new. It stands to reason if you swing something lighter it will travel faster than a heavier weight. Years ago 'light' golf clubs - way, way below say the standard D2 swingweight - were marketed and where are they now? Gone bust.

As we all age we seem to lose distance. Some people faster than others. Last couple of years its been more noticeable for me. Anybody have luck in holding off father time for a little longer? I do work out 4 times a week and strength is not an issue at all.
i saw an interesting take that going lighter is the opposite of what you should do... energy = mass x speed... so as you get slower and go lighter you only compound the problem. It's an idea.
Accept the fact that with age you're going to lose distance, and compensate for it by working hard and sharpening your short game skills

It's all about how much you want 'some' distance back. Yep, you strength train, you can stretch, you can take lessons, you can practice more, you can change clubs and shafts and lofts, etc, etc. As we age, you will lose distance. It's a given. There's not a single guy that hits as long at 65 as he did at 35.

I'm going through this myself to some extent. I work out and am very strong by most measures, but have lost a considerable amount of speed. I saw this video from TPI and they were evaluating an older tour pro to see if he needed strength, speed, or both. After the evaluation, it was determined that he needed speed. For myself, I am spending this winter focusing on quick twitch and speed exercises. All of my core and compound lifts are within 5% of where they were 20 years ago, so I determined that speed was going to be my focus and not power.

I went years into my 40's and never lost distance. Then all of a sudden I did. Not just some distance, but a big amount. In my 16-43 ages I swung driver around 119 and could get it up to 123. Fast forward from 43 and I saw those speeds drop by roughly 2mph per year until almost 48 I was 106-107 with the driver and my 'fast' swing was so unruly I could never hit anything that wasn't 80 yards wide with any consistency. I lost a full 1.5 clubs in iron distance as well.

I lift 6 days a week and speed train now 2 days week. I also lift heavy 5 x5 sets. I stretch after every session for me that is all I can do. I had a severe injury two years ago and could not lift for 8 months. I lost 10 mph ball speed during that time and it just validated all the work I do. I simply cannot afford to not to do it and still compete at the highest level AM levels in the PNW...

I feel you. Terminal illness is no joke. I was on dialysis for 6 years and that took it's toll both physically/mentally. Got my kidney transplant in 2019(godsend). Lost about 5 mph ss which doesn't seem like a lot but when you're a 5hdcp golfer it makes a huge difference.

How long did it take for a normal effort swing to be faster? I completely get swinging faster will produce speed. But speed under control is where I hope i get to. I'm 2 weeks in and tried a max effort drive yesterday and it wasn't pretty...and I play to scratch or better.

Do these sticks make your 90% effort swing produce faster ballspeeds or is it all about max speed for results? I didn't expect my max swing, after 2 weeks, to produce awesome results. But it got me wondering if there is an expected mechanical degradation in the first phase of the protocols for those of us who cannot hit balls more than once a week.

Too many possible reasons to really answer the question. My own guess would be that as you swing harder, your arms are getting so far ahead of the clubhead that the face just isn't squaring up at impact. The faster you swing, the more the problem is exaserbated.

Alot of times it is a rhythm problem. Mechanics as well, but I've never seen you swing...I have a very high swing speed, and while it Looks like I'm swinging out of my shoes sometimes to some people, it is in rhythm and balance. However, a miss hit will be amplified more than a slower swing.

I agree. That's what my instructor tells me. Ever since I've loosened my grip I actually get a nice draw. Mind you I do lose distance. He keeps telling me its better to be 250 in the fairway than 300 in the woods or worst.

I had this problem I slowed down my swing and am hitting straight 280 yards and hitting your 2nd shot from the other fairway. im still getting 240 off the tee and hitting it from my own fairway hit your driver about 3/4ths your normal swing see how it goes

What is the problem? Most people, good and bad alike hit poor shots when they swing out of their shoes. A good player hooks it 15 yards into a fairaway bunker, a higher handicap slices it into the trees, a beginner tops or whiffs completely. Swing nice and easy. Smooth is straight, and straight is long.

When it comes down to it, speed training and stretching will hold off father time for awhile. Something as simple as swinging your driver as fast as possible at the range with 30 balls once a week then swinging your driver with the shaft end as fast as you can at home or in your back yard once a week and stretching every muscle group 4x a week is all you need. Point is, you need to activate your Type II fibers(fast twitch) to maintain ball speed/swing speed or even get faster than you are now.

I've been using the System since December and am now 7 weeks into protocol 1. I'm 64 years old, in good shape, and trying to get back some speed lost over the years. I started at 98-100MPH and after 8 weeks my driver swing speed is pretty consistent at 103-105. On a couple of 'good' days, (I assume my body was in the zone) I was swinging the driver at 106-110.

Thanks for the article. I would be open to try it but actually have a concern about swinging overweight clubs. I recently trained with an overweight club to train my tempo and injured my left elbow. I understand SS training doesn't involve impact but would it be ok to use it with a mild elbow inflammation?

I've noticed some awesome gains and some fractional gain. The BIGGEST thing I've noticed is the sequence and structural gains. The typical golfer who attends one of my golf schools is between 55 and 80 years old and want to recover lost form. Well, speed is something they wish they had back.
Have tried several programs with minimal results. 'May' look into this.

On a more serious note, 250 yards down the middle with a slow swing speed is nothing to sneeze at. Perhaps you need to work your way up to swinging harder, rather than trying to go all out right away.

Think of the 'viking ship' from a carnival. It swings back, kind of pauses briefly at the top, heads back down, and builds speed on the way down to the bottom where it is moving fastest. Same philosophy. Centrifugal force. Swing too fast on the way back and they all fly out all over the park.
if you can hit it straight 250 why would you want to swing faster anyway?
I agree with the poster above, swinging harder usually means using your arms more at the top of your backswing. I do this when i try to hit my driver harder.

You could always take some lessons and see if an instructor could help you get a little more distance. Sometimes you just have to bite the bullet and move up a set of tees. It hurts for a little while but then you realize your back to using the same club on approach shots you used to and get over it.

Yeah, it happens. You could try lighter overall club static weight, work out harder, do more flexibility training instead of weight training. Maybe stronger lofts if you hit it high enough. We all get old. Look at the Senior Tour. You think those guys are hitting it like they used to? But they can still compete.

If you don't do so currently, learn how to hit your driver off the fairway. It's greatly improved my game, and allows me to hit the green on long par 4's that I couldn't reach anymore. It's great fun to watch your playing partner's disbelief when you pull the driver out for an approach, also.
Using stronger lofts doesn't produce a real distance gain. It's the golf club version of a shell game.
Tom Watson's advice to older golfers to help maintain distance is to keep a light grip pressure and make the hands more active in the swing.

I disagree, if you've participated in any level of athletics you'll know that overload in training ultimately leads to the body being primed to overshoot the level at which you'll be competing at in an in-game setting. For example, when I was a football player at a small division 1 school, we would sprint with parachutes that created so much drag, the muscles would be firing for sprinting but had to work 15X harder to achieve minimal success.

While I doubt even with strength and more speed training this winter I will ever get back to 119+, I am happy with where I am, and no longer feel weak. I have gained back most of my driver speed and my irons are actually the same.

I have started to use SuperSpeed and am 4 weeks into the first level. At first I did get on the course and felt like I just wanted to let rip aggressively like I am with the sticks. However, I soon settled down to relax the back swing a little and have definitely developed extra speed swinging through. I am concentrating on stability and swinging aggressively in the training and that seems to work for me.

I'd offer that as a 20+ hcp who can hit his drive 250 and straight, you should spend your time getting the rest of your game to that same high level of performance...and understand, professional golfers notwithstanding, that's a very high level of performance! It will take you to the low single digits if the rest of your game is up to the task. Conversely, if the rest of your game doesn't improve, hitting it 30 yds longer won't make a significant difference in your overall scoring.
